column1: # US State AG AI Enforcement Tracker

column2: US State AG AI Enforcement Tracker (as of 2026-07-18)

column3: Nine state attorney general offices, in United States v. RealPage
column4: California; Colorado; Connecticut; Illinois; Massachusetts; Minnesota; North Carolina; Oregon; Tennessee
column11: Sherman Act Sections 1 and 2 plus state antitrust and consumer-protection claims in the amended RealPage complaint
column14: Settlement filed 2025-11-18; the DOJ RealPage case index lists the Greystar final judgment as entered 2026-03-02.
column15: $7,000,000 aggregate payment to the nine participating states; restrictions on revenue-management products that use competitors' nonpublic, competitively sensitive data; cooperation in the continuing RealPage case.
column16: Nine state attorneys general settled their claims against Greystar in the joint federal and state RealPage action, alleging Greystar used RealPage's shared algorithmic rent-setting system to align rents rather than pricing independently. The settlement is a state disposition distinct from the federal proposed judgments in the same docket.
column17: Algorithmic rent pricing with competitors' nonpublic data

column3: Colorado and North Carolina attorney general offices
column6: Colorado and North Carolina v. Cortland (RealPage action)
column11: Sherman Act and state antitrust claims arising from the RealPage action
column14: Agreement announced 2025-04-11; claims against other defendants in the RealPage action continued.
column15: Cortland agreed not to use nonpublic competitor data through RealPage rent-pricing software and to cooperate in the continuing case. No monetary state payment was reported in the Colorado release.
column16: Colorado and North Carolina reached an agreement with Cortland resolving state claims that Cortland participated in rent-setting through RealPage's algorithm-driven system using landlords' private rental data.
column17: Algorithmic rent pricing with competitors' nonpublic data

column3: Arizona Office of the Attorney General
column5: RealPage, Inc. and nine residential landlords
column11: Arizona Uniform State Antitrust Act; Arizona Consumer Fraud Act
column14: Standalone Arizona lawsuit announced 2024-02-28. Arizona is not listed among the plaintiff states in the joint DOJ RealPage case.
column15: Injunctive and monetary relief sought; no resolution verified as of the last check.
column16: Arizona sued RealPage and nine major residential landlords operating in Arizona, alleging they conspired to raise rents by replacing independent pricing decisions with software-generated recommendations built on competitors' confidential pricing and occupancy data.
column17: Algorithmic rent pricing with competitors' nonpublic data

column3: Massachusetts Office of the Attorney General
column11: Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act, M.G.L. c. 93A, and fair-lending laws cited by the AGO
column14: Settled through an assurance of discontinuance filed in Suffolk Superior Court; compliance and reporting obligations remain ongoing.
column15: $2,500,000 payment to Massachusetts; AI governance and compliance policies; ongoing reporting to the AGO; discontinuation of the Cohort Default Rate variable and an immigration-status Knockout Rule in underwriting models.
column16: The Massachusetts AGO alleged that Earnest's AI and algorithmic student-loan underwriting models created unmitigated risks of disparate harm to Black, Hispanic, and non-citizen applicants and borrowers. The assurance of discontinuance imposes model-variable, governance, and reporting remedies alongside the payment.
column17: AI loan underwriting with alleged discriminatory effects

column3: New Hampshire Department of Justice, Election Law Unit
column6: New Hampshire cease-and-desist to Life Corporation
column14: Cease-and-desist order issued 2024-02-06 with a continuing investigation announced; no later state monetary penalty against Life Corporation was located.
column15: State cease-and-desist order; the parallel 51-attorney-general task force warning and later federal FCC actions produced separate relief.
column16: New Hampshire issued a cease-and-desist order to Life Corporation after robocalls carrying an AI-generated voice clone of President Biden told recipients to save their vote for November rather than participate in the January 23, 2024 presidential primary.
column17: AI voice-clone robocalls aimed at deterring voting

column11: Felony voter-suppression charges under N.H. RSA 659:40 and misdemeanor candidate-impersonation charges
column14: Charged 2024-05-23 with 13 felony voter-suppression and 13 misdemeanor candidate-impersonation counts; contemporaneous reporting states the consolidated trial proceeded on 11 counts of each and the jury acquitted Kramer on all counts tried on 2025-06-13.
column15: State criminal prosecution ended in acquittal. The FCC's separate $6 million forfeiture against Kramer was a distinct federal action and was not vacated by the state verdict.
column16: New Hampshire prosecuted the political consultant who commissioned the AI-generated Biden voice-clone robocalls before the 2024 presidential primary. The acquittal illustrates the difficulty of fitting AI voice-cloning conduct into older state criminal statutes even where parallel federal telecom remedies succeed.
column17: AI voice-clone robocalls aimed at deterring voting

column3: Texas Office of the Attorney General
column11: Texas Deceptive Trade Practices-Consumer Protection Act, Tex. Bus. & Com. Code Sections 17.41-17.63
column14: Assurance of voluntary compliance; the petition for approval was file-stamped 2024-08-21 and the settlement was announced 2024-09-18. Conduct obligations run five years from the effective date.
column15: Five years of conduct obligations: disclose definitions and calculation methods for performance metrics or use independent auditing; avoid false or unsubstantiated statements; disclose known risks, limitations, training information, and appropriate uses to customers; and provide sufficient user documentation. No monetary payment was identified.
column16: Texas alleged that Pieces marketed its hospital generative-AI summarization products with inaccurate or insufficiently substantiated accuracy claims, including a severe-hallucination rate below one per 100,000 outputs. Texas described the matter as a first-of-its-kind healthcare generative-AI investigation.
column17: Unsubstantiated accuracy claims for healthcare generative AI

column3: Texas Office of the Attorney General
column11: Texas Capture or Use of Biometric Identifier Act, Tex. Bus. & Com. Code Section 503.001; Texas DTPA
column14: Settlement announced 2024-07-30; the suit was filed in February 2022 and is included because it resolved inside the tracker window.
column15: $1.4 billion payable to Texas over five years, announced by the Texas AG as the largest settlement ever obtained from an action brought by a single state.
column16: Texas alleged that Facebook ran facial-recognition software on photographs uploaded by users and captured Texans' facial geometry without the notice and consent required by CUBI. The 2024 settlement resolved the state's biometric and DTPA claims.
column17: Facial-recognition capture of biometric identifiers without consent

column3: Texas Office of the Attorney General
column11: Texas Capture or Use of Biometric Identifier Act and DTPA claims, combined with separate state privacy claims
column14: Announced 2025-05-09 as a settlement resolving two Texas privacy lawsuits; later Texas OAG materials describe the suits as settled. A final judgment or complete settlement agreement has not been published.
column15: $1.375 billion aggregate settlement covering two Texas privacy lawsuits. The announcement did not publish detailed conduct terms.
column16: Texas alleged that Google captured or analyzed voiceprints and facial geometry through products including Google Photos, Google Assistant, and Nest familiar-faces functionality without legally sufficient consent, alongside separate geolocation and incognito-mode privacy claims.
column17: Voiceprint and facial-geometry capture without consent
